name: Negligent Monitoring Co-Defendant Exposure
description: Reversal 5 litigation-cascade evaluation for the Negligent Monitoring Risk Agent: assesses ClearFlow Health Network's co-defendant exposure under negligent-monitoring theories and whether a grandchild-case privilege reshuffle is required. Covers high-exposure, low-exposure, and privilege-reshuffle scenarios.

evaluations.name: High exposure - squarely named co-defendant with material monitoring gaps

inputs.litigation_event: A consolidated provider action names ClearFlow Health Network as a co-defendant alongside Nimbus Patient Engagement Platform under a negligent-monitoring theory. Plaintiffs (Northstar Regional Health, Provider Alpha, and Provider Gamma) allege ClearFlow breached a cross-customer detection duty by failing to correlate the shared Nimbus vendor compromise across its provider customers, allowing claim-flow anomalies to persist for weeks.
inputs.monitoring_history: ClearFlow's claim-flow anomaly detection flagged isolated drops at Northstar, Provider Alpha, and Provider Gamma on overlapping dates but the cross-customer pattern correlation was not run until 18 days after the first anomaly. No standing cross-customer correlation control existed at the time; the multi-customer pattern detector was activated only after the third provider reported. Detection telemetry shows material gaps in the shared-vendor monitoring layer.

expectedOutput.rationale: Decision-support draft for counsel review, not legal advice. ClearFlow is squarely named as a co-defendant alongside Nimbus under a negligent-monitoring theory that targets its cross-customer detection duty. The monitoring history shows material gaps: an 18-day correlation delay and no standing cross-customer detection control, which strengthens the theory against ClearFlow. Exposure is high. Because ClearFlow is named as a co-defendant, a privilege reshuffle is required: grandchild-case work product across the affected BAA-obligation and regulator-response cases should move from operational to attorney-client / work-product classification under Hawthorne Mercer LLP direction, particularly the cross-customer correlation analyses and internal detection-gap assessments.

evaluations.name: Low exposure - nominal naming, defensible monitoring controls, no co-defendant designation

inputs.litigation_event: A single-provider complaint references ClearFlow Health Network only in background recitals naming Nimbus Patient Engagement Platform as the primary defendant for the vendor compromise. ClearFlow is mentioned as the payment intermediary but is not designated a co-defendant; the negligent-monitoring theory is directed at Nimbus's own platform controls, not at ClearFlow's cross-customer detection.
inputs.monitoring_history: ClearFlow's claim-flow anomaly detection identified the anomaly at Provider Beta within 24 hours and ran cross-customer pattern correlation the same day under a standing control, escalating to the shared-vendor review immediately. Detection telemetry shows timely, documented controls with no material gaps.

expectedOutput.rationale: Decision-support draft for counsel review, not legal advice. ClearFlow is named only nominally in background recitals and is not designated a co-defendant; the negligent-monitoring theory targets Nimbus's platform controls rather than ClearFlow's cross-customer detection duty. The monitoring history shows reasonable, timely controls (24-hour detection, same-day standing correlation, documented escalation) with no material gaps, making the theory weak against ClearFlow. Exposure is low. Because ClearFlow is not named as a co-defendant, no privilege reshuffle is required and grandchild-case work product may remain in operational classification.

evaluations.name: Privilege reshuffle - plausible co-defendant triggers reclassification

inputs.litigation_event: An amended complaint adds ClearFlow Health Network as a co-defendant alongside Nimbus Patient Engagement Platform under a negligent-monitoring theory. Plaintiffs Provider Delta and Provider Epsilon allege ClearFlow had gaps in its shared-vendor monitoring, though they concede ClearFlow maintained some detection practices. The co-defendant designation triggers outside-counsel review of case work-product classification.
inputs.monitoring_history: ClearFlow's anomaly detection flagged the Provider Delta and Provider Epsilon drops within several days and ran cross-customer correlation, but the shared-vendor monitoring layer had a partial coverage gap that delayed linking the two providers to the Nimbus compromise. Defensible detection practices existed, but the monitoring history is mixed with documented gaps.

expectedOutput.rationale: Decision-support draft for counsel review, not legal advice. ClearFlow is added as a plausible co-defendant alongside Nimbus under a negligent-monitoring theory. The monitoring history is mixed: cross-customer correlation was run within days and defensible detection practices existed, but a partial shared-vendor coverage gap delayed linking Provider Delta and Provider Epsilon to the Nimbus compromise. This supports a moderate exposure assessment rather than high. Because the amended complaint names ClearFlow as a co-defendant, a privilege reshuffle is required: grandchild-case work product tied to the Provider Delta and Provider Epsilon BAA-obligation cases should move from operational to attorney-client / work-product classification under Hawthorne Mercer LLP direction, including the detection-gap analyses and shared-vendor monitoring assessments.
